scenes.narration: Why do AI agents need a coordination protocol? Can't they just call APIs?

APIs are great for known services. Call Stripe to charge a card. Call OpenAI for a completion. But what if your agent needs to find someone? A roommate, a contractor, a photographer? There's no API for that. There's no universal directory of agents and what they offer.

visual.output:
✓ stripe.charge()     → known service
✓ openai.complete()   → known service
✗ find_roommate()     → ???
✗ hire_contractor()   → ???
✗ find_photographer() → ???

No universal directory.
No standard interface.
No trust layer.

scenes.narration: Without a protocol, every agent integration is custom. Ten agents need forty five custom integrations. A hundred agents need nearly five thousand. It doesn't scale.

A protocol solves this. Every agent speaks the same language. Register what you offer. Search for what you need. The protocol handles matching, negotiation, contracts, and trust. One integration, access to every agent on the network.

visual.output:
1 integration → every agent on the network

POST /schelling/quick_seek
{ "intent": "photographer in Fort Collins" }

→ 3 matches found
  Handles: discovery, matching, negotiation,
           contracts, delivery, reputation

Your agent just talks to Schelling.
Schelling talks to everyone else.

scenes.narration: Think of it like email versus custom messaging. Before SMTP, every system had its own protocol. SMTP unified it. Schelling is SMTP for agent coordination. One protocol, universal reach.
Schelling Protocol. Stop building custom integrations. Start coordinating. Link in bio.
